topic: I bought a thrift store film camera with an undeveloped roll inside and the photos are of my own apartment dated three weeks before I moved in
title: I Bought a Thrift Store Film Camera. The Roll Inside Holds Photos of My Apartment from Before I Moved In. | True Scary Story
description_intro: I bought a Pentax K1000 film camera at a thrift store in Minneapolis last August for twelve dollars. The roll of Kodak Gold inside the camera had twenty four exposures already shot. I developed the roll at a local lab. Every photograph on the roll is the interior of my own apartment. The date stamp on the negatives reads three weeks before I moved in. I have lived in the apartment alone for six years.
description:
I bought a Pentax K1000 film camera at a thrift store in Minneapolis last August for twelve dollars. The roll of Kodak Gold inside the camera had twenty four exposures already shot. I developed the roll at a local lab. Every photograph on the roll is the interior of my own apartment. The date stamp on the negatives reads three weeks before I moved in.

The thrift store was a small charity shop on Lyndale Avenue called Second Bloom. The donations log listed the camera as a drop off from a woman named Margaret Helle from Saint Paul. Margaret is eighty two years old. She lives in an assisted living facility called Crescent Pines. I visited her in September. She remembered the camera. She remembered the apartment in the photographs. She told me she had once had a roommate who looked exactly like me.

There is one photograph in the set that I have only shown my landlord. The photograph is taken from inside my bedroom closet looking out toward the bed. There is a figure asleep on the bed. The figure is me. The photograph was developed from film that had been sitting in the camera for nine years. Watch to the end. The photographs are currently in a manila folder in the closet shown in the final image.

#scarystories #truescarystories #horrornarration #analoghorror #scarystoriestotellinthedark #truehorrorstories #creepypasta #foundtapes
tags: scary stories to tell in the dark

sections.narration: I bought a Pentax K1000 film camera at a thrift store in Minneapolis last August for twelve dollars. The light meter still worked. The shutter was crisp. I opened the back to check the film door seal and saw there was a roll already loaded. The frame counter showed twenty four. The roll had been completely exposed and never advanced past the final frame. I closed the back carefully and dropped the camera at a local development lab the next morning. Two days later the lab called me. They asked if I had loaded the film myself. I said I had not. They said the negatives were photographs of someone's home interior. They asked if I wanted to come look.

sections.narration: Second Bloom is a small charity thrift store on Lyndale Avenue. The shop benefits a hospice in Saint Paul. The owner is a woman named Beverly Tan who has run it for twenty years. She keeps a paper donation log for every item that comes in. The camera had been donated in late July. The donor was listed in the log as Margaret Helle. Address Crescent Pines assisted living facility. Saint Paul Minnesota. Beverly remembered Margaret. She said Margaret had brought in a single cardboard box of household items. The camera. A photograph album. Some books. Margaret had not stayed to shop. She had handed Beverly the box and walked back to the car a nephew was waiting in.

sections.narration: I have been buying old film cameras for ten years. I have about thirty of them on a bookshelf in my apartment. Pentax. Olympus. Canon. Nikon. Most were thrift store finds or estate sale buys. I shoot a roll through each one to test it and then I shelve it. I do not sell them. I drink coffee in the morning and process my negatives on weekends. The Pentax K1000 is a common camera. They are easy to find in working condition. The reason I bought this one was the light meter. Most of them go bad over the decades. This one was still calibrated. I expected to shoot a test roll. I did not expect to develop someone else's.

sections.narration: The lab tech was a man named Aaron Cortez. He had been processing film for fourteen years. He laid out a contact sheet on the light table when I walked in. Twenty four small prints arranged in four rows of six. The first frame was a living room. Worn hardwood floor. A bay window with curtains pulled to one side. A green couch. I stood looking at the print for a long time. The couch on the contact sheet was mine. The couch I had bought from a furniture store in Saint Paul three years ago. The room around it was my own living room. The angle of the photograph was from a corner of the room I sit in often.
image_prompts: wide interior of a small film development lab with a light table covered in printed contact sheets, dim ambient light, no people visible, horizontal 16:9 framing

sections.narration: I went home and matched every frame. Twenty four photographs. Twenty four rooms or corners of rooms in my apartment. The kitchen with my exact cabinets. The bedroom with the bed in the same position I sleep in. The bathroom with my own framed print above the toilet. The hallway with the carpet I had picked out at a flooring store. The angle of every photograph was a place I had stood or sat or walked through. The lighting in the photographs was natural daylight. The walls were painted the same color as mine. The Pentax had been loaded by someone who knew my home well. The camera had been donated by a woman in an assisted living facility.

sections.narration: I asked Aaron to enlarge one of the negatives so I could read the date stamp printed on the frame. Older film backs have a date imprint feature. The Pentax K1000 does not. The date was printed on the negative itself. Aaron called it a film stock factory mark. Kodak Gold of a certain era was emulsion stamped with the year of expiration. The expiration year on the roll was two thousand sixteen. He calculated the shooting date as approximately two thousand fifteen based on the chemistry. I moved into my apartment in October two thousand sixteen. The film had been shot in the months before my move. The negatives had been sitting in the camera ever since.

sections.narration: I drove to Crescent Pines in Saint Paul the following weekend. Margaret Helle was eighty two. She lived in a small private suite with a window that looked out on a courtyard garden. The nurse who let me in said Margaret had good days and bad days. This was a good day. I sat across from her at her small table and showed her the contact sheet. She studied each print carefully with a magnifying glass. She told me she had once lived in the apartment in the photographs. She had moved out in two thousand fifteen because her doctor recommended assisted care. She had taken the photographs herself with the Pentax in the months before she left.

sections.narration: I told Margaret I had moved into the apartment in October two thousand sixteen. She told me that was about a year after she had moved out. She said the apartment had been empty for a long time before she sold it. The previous tenant had been her roommate of forty years. Margaret had been a public school librarian. Her roommate had been a piano tuner who traveled for work. She said her roommate had looked exactly like me. She did not mean similar. She did not mean a resemblance. She told me her roommate had my face. My exact face. She said his name was Henrik. Henrik Larsen. He had disappeared from the apartment in the autumn of nineteen seventy three.

sections.narration: Margaret asked me to find frame seventeen on the contact sheet. I found it. It was the photograph I had been avoiding since Aaron handed me the prints. The angle was from inside my bedroom closet looking out toward the bed. The closet door was open just a few inches. The bed was made. Someone was asleep on top of the covers. The face on the pillow was visible in profile. The face was mine. Margaret told me Henrik had taken the photograph the night before he disappeared. He had set the camera on a closet shelf with a self timer. He had wanted a photograph of himself sleeping. He had told her he wanted to see his own face from outside.

sections.narration: I keep the contact sheet and the negatives in a manila folder in my bedroom closet. The folder sits on the same shelf the camera was set on for frame seventeen. I have not photographed anything in my apartment since. I have not opened the closet at night. Margaret died in March. The funeral was small. She left a final letter for me with her nephew. The letter said only this. Henrik came back to the apartment three times after he disappeared. She had not been there for two of the visits. She had been there for the third. He had taken a photograph of her sleeping. The photograph is on the contact sheet. I had not noticed her in frame eleven until last week.
